data.output:
1	---
2	title: Principles
3	weight: 2
4	---
5	
6	DDx is shaped by a small set of load-bearing principles. They drive the
7	trade-offs you see across the CLI, the bead tracker, and the agent service.
8	Each principle here is one we cite in real design and review decisions — if it
9	weren't, it wouldn't be on the page.
10	
11	## Platform, Not Methodology
12	
13	DDx provides primitives. Workflow tools (HELIX) and other consumers
14	provide opinions. The CLI owns the document library, bead tracker, agent
15	dispatch, personas, templates, and git sync. Phases, gates, supervisory loops,
16	and methodology validation belong in plugins.
17	
18	**In practice:** when someone proposes adding a "frame phase" command or a
19	"requirements gate" check to `ddx`, the answer is no — those live in the HELIX
20	plugin. When two workflow plugins need the same primitive (say, content
21	hashing), it gets lifted into DDx.
22	
23	## Project-Local by Default
24	
25	`ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only write under `<projectRoot>`. There
26	is no home-directory state and no machine-wide opinions. The single global
27	artifact is `ddx-server`. Per-user concerns (API keys, model preferences) are
28	the user's responsibility, not DDx's install surface.
29	
30	**In practice:** a project's plugin tree lives at `<projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/`,
31	its skills at `<projectRoot>/.claude/skills/`, its bead store inside the repo.
32	Cloning the repo gives you the whole picture; nothing on the developer's
33	laptop changes the answer.
34	
35	## Documents Are the Product
36	
37	Code is output. Documents are what you maintain. Prompts, personas, patterns,
38	templates, and specs are first-class, versioned, agent-consumable artifacts —
39	not afterthoughts you write once and forget.
40	
41	**In practice:** when a spec changes, beads are created for the work the
42	change implies. When implementation reveals the spec was wrong, the spec is
43	updated and the implementation follows. The artifact hierarchy is a set of
44	lenses, not a one-way pipeline.
45	
46	## Beads Are the Unit of Work
47	
48	Work flows through beads — self-contained items with acceptance criteria and
49	a dependency DAG. The queue is the interface between intent and execution.
50	Anything that needs to happen should be expressible as a bead; anything an
51	agent does should be against a bead.
52	
53	**In practice:** a developer noticing a bug doesn't fix it inline — they
54	`ddx bead create`, link dependencies, and either pick it up or let
55	`ddx work` drain the queue. The bead is the contract; review checks the
56	acceptance criteria, not the developer's memory of the conversation.
57	
58	## Cost-Tiered Throughput
59	
60	Optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability. Cheap models do; strong
61	models review; deterministic checks sit at the top of the ladder catching
62	what review missed. Routing is by capability and endpoint, not by
63	hardcoded provider name.
64	
65	**In practice:** `ddx try` runs implementation on a cheap model in an
66	isolated worktree, then routes the diff to a stronger reviewer; `ddx work`
67	drains the queue across many attempts. Failed reviews escalate to a higher
68	tier on the next attempt. The agent service discovers models from endpoints
69	rather than wiring named providers.
70	
71	## Git-Native, File-First
72	
73	Plain files. Standard git. No lock-in. Documents live in the repo, beads live
74	in the repo, plugins live in the repo. Sync is `git pull` and `git push` —
75	not a proprietary protocol.
76	
77	**In practice:** beads are JSONL on disk; you can read them with `cat`,
78	diff them, and merge them. Library documents are Markdown and YAML; you can
79	edit them in any tool. There is no DDx server you must connect to.
80	
81	## Agent-Agnostic
82	
83	Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out contract plugs into the agent
84	service. Claude, Codex, Gemini, local models — all use the same prompt
85	envelope, the same session logging, the same quorum review.
86	
87	**In practice:** `ddx agent run --harness claude` and `ddx agent run --harness
88	qwen-local` differ in cost and capability, not in interface. Routing decisions
89	are observable, swappable, and don't require touching the CLI.
90	
91	## Make Intent Explicit
92	
93	Code, configs, and documents should say what they mean. Avoid implicit
94	conventions, magic values, and behavior that depends on undocumented ordering.
95	When intent is ambiguous, name it.
96	
97	**In practice:** persona bindings are explicit in `.ddx.yml`, not inferred
98	from filenames. Bead acceptance criteria are checked items, not prose
99	expectations. A reviewer can tell what was intended without asking.
100	
101	## Prefer Reversible Decisions
102	
103	When two options are otherwise equivalent, choose the one easier to undo.
104	Commit to irreversible choices deliberately, with documented rationale.
105	
106	**In practice:** endpoint-based agent routing is preferred over hardcoded
107	provider names because endpoints are swappable. Project-local install is
108	preferred over global state because it's reversible by deleting `.ddx/`.
109	
110	## When Principles Conflict
111	
112	Apply the one whose violation would cause the worse outcome in the current
113	context. Document the tension in the commit message or design note. Common
114	tensions:
115	
116	- **Platform vs. Simplicity** — keeping plugins out of the CLI sometimes
117	  costs duplication across plugins. Accept the duplication until two plugins
118	  prove the primitive is shared infrastructure.
119	- **Cost-Tiered vs. Reversible Decisions** — routing logic that locks in a
120	  vendor is cheap to ship and expensive to undo. Prefer endpoint abstractions
121	  even when a single provider would be simpler today.
122	
123	For the canonical version of these principles (terse, contributor-facing),
124	see `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md` in the repository.
125

1	---
2	title: Principles
3	weight: 3
4	---
5	
6	The ten domain principles that shape DDx as a document-driven software
7	factory. Each page expands the principle, summarizes the evidence behind
8	it, and names the DDx mechanism that enacts it.
9	
10	The principles are grounded in research syntheses RSCH-001 through
11	RSCH-010 in `docs/helix/00-discover/research/` and the references they
12	cite in `docs/helix/00-discover/references/`.
13	
14	## The ten
15	
16	1. [Spec-first development](spec-first-development/) — specs, not code, are the durable artifact.
17	2. [Executable specifications](executable-specifications/) — a spec that cannot be checked is a wish.
18	3. [Audit trail required](audit-trail-required/) — every artifact traces to the inputs that caused it.
19	4. [Context is king](context-is-king/) — the brief is the lever on quality and cost.
20	5. [Work is a DAG](work-is-a-dag/) — dependencies are explicit edges, not prose.
21	6. [Right-size the model](right-size-the-model/) — cheapest tool that does the job; escalate on failure.
22	7. [Avoid vendor lock-in](avoid-vendor-lock-in/) — standardize the contract, swap the implementation.
23	8. [Drift is debt](drift-is-debt/) — divergence between spec, code, and config compounds.
24	9. [Least privilege for agents](least-privilege-for-agents/) — scope file and tool surface to the task.
25	10. [Inspect and adapt](inspect-and-adapt/) — evidence-bound generate-then-review loops, not generic retros.
26	
27	For the canonical short form, see `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md` in
28	the repository.
29

1 --- 2 title: Concepts 3 weight: 2 4 --- 5 6 The ideas behind DDx and document-driven development. 7 8 ## What DDx Is 9 10 DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is the **shared infrastructure 11 platform for document-driven development**. It provides the primitives that 12 developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and deliver the 13 documents AI agents consume to build software. 14 15 The core thesis: **document quality, not agent capability, is the bottleneck.** 16 Well-maintained abstractions — requirements, architecture, design, tests — 17 produce better software than working at the code level alone. DDx encodes that 18 insight into infrastructure. 19 20 ```mermaid 21 flowchart LR 22 Dev["Developer
(maintains documents)"] 23 subgraph Docs["Documents (the product)"] 24 direction TB 25 Specs["Specs"] 26 Personas["Personas"] 27 Patterns["Patterns"] 28 Templates["Templates"] 29 Prompts["Prompts"] 30 end 31 Agent["AI Agent
(harness)"] 32 Code["Code,
tests, artifacts"] 33 34 Dev -->|edits| Docs 35 Docs -->|composed into prompt| Agent 36 Agent -->|produces| Code 37 Code -.->|feedback informs| Docs 38 39 classDef product fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937; 40 class Docs product; 41 ``` 42 43 ## Platform and Workflow 44 45 DDx is the platform layer; workflow tools sit on top with explicit boundaries. 46 Each layer is independently useful and replaceable. 47 48 | Layer | Project | Owns | 49 |-------|---------|------| 50 | **Platform** | [DDx](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync | 51 | **Workflow** | [HELIX](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/helix) | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, methodology | 52 53 DDx provides primitives. HELIX and other consumers provide opinions. 54 55 ```mermaid 56 flowchart TB 57 subgraph Workflow["Workflow layer — opinions"] 58 HELIX["HELIX
phases · gates · supervisory dispatch"] 59 Other["...alternative
workflows"] 60 end 61 subgraph Platform["Platform layer — primitives"] 62 DDx["DDx
document library · bead tracker ·
agent dispatch · personas · git sync"] 63 end 64 65 HELIX -->|consumes primitives| DDx 66 Other -->|consumes primitives| DDx 67 68 classDef platform fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e293b; 69 classDef workflow fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#1e293b; 70 class DDx platform; 71 class HELIX,Other workflow; 72 ``` 73 74 {{< callout type="info" >}} 75 **Why split platform from workflow?** Mixing methodology into platform code 76 locks teams into one way of working. Keeping the platform opinion-free lets 77 HELIX evolve, lets alternative workflows exist, and lets DDx serve both 78 fully-autonomous and human-driven teams from the same primitives. 79 {{< /callout >}} 80 81 ## Documents Are the Product 82 83 The fundamental shift: **you maintain documents, agents produce code.** 84 85 In traditional development, code is the primary artifact. In document-driven 86 development, the primary artifacts are the documents that tell agents what to 87 build and how to build it: 88 89 - **Prompts** — instructions that direct agent behavior for specific tasks 90 - **Personas** — behavioral definitions that shape how agents approach work 91 - **Patterns** — proven solutions to recurring problems, written for agent 92 consumption 93 - **Templates** — project and file blueprints with variable substitution 94 - **Specs** — requirements and designs that define what to build 95 96 The quality of agent output follows directly from the quality of these 97 documents. Better documents produce better code, every time. 98 99 ## What DDx Provides 100 101 - **Document library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts 102 in the repository. 103 - **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues, 104 and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute. 105 - **Agent service** — unified harness dispatch (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local 106 models) with quorum review, session logging, and a single prompt envelope. 107 - **Execute-loop** — drain the bead queue with isolated worktrees, automatic 108 review, and recovery. Cheap models do, strong models review. 109 - **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install ` only touch 110 ``. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`. 111 - **Single `ddx` skill** — one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One surface 112 for agents to learn. 113 114 ## What DDx Is Not 115 116 - **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types — 117 workflow tools own those. 118 - **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend. 119 - **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever you already work. 120 - **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out 121 contract plugs in. 122 123 ## Read Next 124 125 - [Principles](principles/) — the load-bearing decisions behind DDx. 126 - [Architecture](architecture/) — how beads, personas, and the 127 project-local install model fit together. 128 - [Run Architecture](run-architecture/) — the layered `ddx run` / 129 `ddx try` / `ddx work` model that drains the bead queue. 130 - [Glossary](glossary/) — quick definitions for the terms used across the 131 docs. 132

input.content:
---
title: Principles
weight: 2
aliases:
  - /docs/concepts/principles/
---

The load-bearing principles behind DDx now live under
[/docs/principles/](/docs/principles/). Each has its own page expanding
the principle, the evidence behind it, and the DDx mechanism that enacts it.

## The ten principles

1. [Spec-first development](/docs/principles/spec-first-development/) — specs, not code, are the durable artifact.
2. [Executable specifications](/docs/principles/executable-specifications/) — a spec that cannot be checked is a wish.
3. [Audit trail required](/docs/principles/audit-trail-required/) — every artifact traces to the inputs that caused it.
4. [Context is king](/docs/principles/context-is-king/) — the brief is the lever on quality and cost.
5. [Work is a DAG](/docs/principles/work-is-a-dag/) — dependencies are explicit edges, not prose.
6. [Right-size the model](/docs/principles/right-size-the-model/) — cheapest tool that does the job; escalate on failure.
7. [Avoid vendor lock-in](/docs/principles/avoid-vendor-lock-in/) — standardize the contract, swap the implementation.
8. [Drift is debt](/docs/principles/drift-is-debt/) — divergence between spec, code, and config compounds.
9. [Least privilege for agents](/docs/principles/least-privilege-for-agents/) — scope file and tool surface to the task.
10. [Inspect and adapt](/docs/principles/inspect-and-adapt/) — evidence-bound generate-then-review loops, not generic retros.

For the canonical short form, see `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md` in the repository.

data.text: Replaced `website/content/docs/concepts/principles.md` with a thin umbrella that lists and links to the ten new `/docs/principles/<slug>/` pages, with a Hugo alias for the old path. Hugo builds cleanly (no 404 warnings).
